title: Carved ash salad spoon
subtitle: A long flat-bowled serving spoon in green ash
excerpt: A flat-bowled green-ash salad spoon with a broad shallow scoop and a comfortable handle. Lighter and longer than a soup spoon. Good second project after a first eating spoon: bowl hollowing is shallower and faster.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for blank-shaping method. Treen form documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and the Museum of English Rural Life open-access treen archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.

projectSchedule.title: Carve the salad spoon
projectSchedule.body: Shape the blank and hollow the shallow bowl.

projectSchedule.title: Spoon is cured and food-safe
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks have passed since oiling. The salad spoon is ready to use.

content.text: A salad spoon has a broad, shallow bowl and a long handle, carved from a single piece of
green wood

. Ash is a good choice: the grain is open and slightly coarser than sycamore, which makes it a touch more challenging to finish cleanly, but it dries food-safe and the pale colour with subtle grain is appealing. A 28 cm long billet, 6 cm across, gives a finished spoon roughly 26 cm long.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Ash carves readily when green and the open grain takes oil well. Source from windfall, from a tree surgeon's off-cuts, or from a coppice. The grain runs in long straight lines that are easy to read. Cuts with the grain are clean. Cuts against the grain in ash lift fibres rather than chips, so grain direction matters more here than in sycamore.
If ash is not available, sycamore is the easier substitute. Cherry also works and gives a warmer colour when dry.
Shaping the blank
The salad-spoon
blank

is a flat billet with one convex face (the back of the bowl) and a long handle. Use push cuts with the carving axe to rough out the handle taper and to reduce the bowl section to the right depth. Keep the bowl section 8 mm thick at this stage for the hook-knife work.

Switch to the sloyd knife for the outside profile. Push cuts along the handle narrow it to an oval cross-section. The back of the bowl should be gently convex: a slight dome of 6 mm from rim to centre. This dome is what gives the spoon its scoop shape.
Hollowing the bowl

The salad-spoon bowl is shallow: 6 to 8 mm of depth from rim to the deepest point. Use the medium hook knife in short arcs from the centre outward. Work your way around the bowl evenly. The grain in ash runs along the spoon length; cuts across the width of the bowl (across the grain) need a slicing action at an angle, not a straight-in push. Angling the blade at 30 degrees to the cut direction reduces resistance on cross-grain strokes.
Finishing

Let the spoon dry for two to three weeks in a paper bag. Sand lightly with 400 grit, then apply raw linseed oil inside and out. Wipe off the surplus after one hour. Two weeks cure before food contact.
Variations
Matched pair: carve two from the same billet so the grain pattern and colour match. One for serving, one for tossing.
Slotted version: once dry, bore three small holes through the bowl section with a brace and bit. The holes drain dressing from the leaves when tossing.
Care
Wash by hand, towel-dry. No dishwasher, no long soaking. Re-oil once or twice a year.

rows.symptom: Hook knife tears the bowl surface in some directions
rows.cause: Cutting against the open grain of ash
rows.fix: Approach from the opposite direction. In ash, the grain direction matters more than in sycamore because the fibres are coarser. Reverse and use a slicing action at an angle.

rows.symptom: Bowl is deeper on one side than the other
rows.cause: Working unevenly around the bowl perimeter
rows.fix: Check by pressing a straight edge across the rim. Mark the high areas and take more from those sections only.
